one_liner: A 32-year-old man presents with 3 days of sharp, pleuritic chest pain that worsens when lying flat and improves when sitting forward; he had an upper respiratory infection the week before.

stage2.hpi_extension: The pain is sharp, substernal, and radiates to the left trapezius ridge. It is worse with deep inspiration and when supine, and it improves when he sits up and leans forward. He had a cough and low-grade fever last week that has since resolved. He denies exertional chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, or radiation to the jaw or arm. No tobacco, drug use, or recent travel.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student, non-smoker, no drugs, occasional alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: Young man leaning forward on the exam table. Cardiac: **triphasic pericardial friction rub** heard best at the left sternal border with the patient leaning forward at end-expiration (atrial systole, ventricular systole, and early diastolic filling components). No murmurs. No JVD. Lungs clear. Pain **reproduced by lying supine**, **relieved by sitting up and leaning forward**. No peripheral edema; pulses equal bilaterally.

lab_results.result: **Diffuse concave-upward ST elevations in most leads (I, II, III, aVL, aVF, V2–V6)** with reciprocal ST depression only in aVR and V1. **PR depression in the same leads** and **PR elevation in aVR**. No Q waves.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Diffuse concave ST elevations with PR depression (and PR elevation in aVR) are the classic EKG findings of acute pericarditis, reflecting inflammation of the visceral pericardium. In contrast, STEMI shows localized ST elevations with reciprocal changes.

lab_results.result: **ESR 58 mm/hr (elevated)**. **CRP 6.2 mg/dL (elevated)**. WBC 11,500 with mild lymphocytic predominance. Troponin mildly elevated at 0.08 (myopericarditis).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated ESR and CRP support an acute inflammatory process. Mildly elevated troponin suggests concurrent myocardial involvement (myopericarditis), which is common and generally carries a good prognosis.

lab_results.result: Transthoracic echocardiogram: **small circumferential pericardial effusion** (8 mm) without evidence of tamponade. Normal LV and RV function. No regional wall motion abnormalities.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A small pericardial effusion is present in ~60% of acute pericarditis cases. Echocardiogram excludes tamponade physiology (chamber collapse, respiratory variation in inflow).

stage5.final_clue: He is discharged on high-dose ibuprofen plus colchicine for 3 months; colchicine reduces the risk of recurrence by nearly half compared to NSAIDs alone.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acute pericarditis classically presents with sharp, pleuritic, substernal chest pain that is **positional** — worse when supine and improved by sitting up and leaning forward — often radiating to the trapezius ridge (innervated by the phrenic nerve). Exam reveals a **triphasic pericardial friction rub**, and EKG shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. Many cases follow a viral prodrome.
debrief.pathophysiology: Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardial sac. Etiologies: **idiopathic/viral (most common, usually Coxsackievirus B)**, bacterial (TB, especially in developing countries), autoimmune (SLE, RA), uremia, post-MI (early fibrinous pericarditis or late **Dressler syndrome** 2–10 weeks post-MI, an autoimmune phenomenon), malignancy, and radiation. Complications include pericardial effusion, cardiac tamponade, and chronic **constrictive pericarditis** with fibrosis and calcification ('tuberculous pericarditis' is a classic cause of constriction).
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is a high-dose **NSAID (ibuprofen or aspirin)** plus **colchicine** for 3 months. Colchicine reduces recurrence by ~50%. Aspirin is preferred over NSAIDs in post-MI pericarditis (other NSAIDs may impair healing). Glucocorticoids are reserved for refractory cases or autoimmune etiologies because they increase recurrence risk. Treat the underlying cause (dialysis for uremic, antibiotics for bacterial). Monitor for tamponade. Avoid exercise until symptoms and biomarkers normalize.
high_yield_pearls: **Pericarditis pain** = sharp, pleuritic, positional (worse supine, better sitting forward), radiates to the trapezius.
**EKG**: diffuse concave-up ST elevations with **PR depression** (and reciprocal PR elevation in aVR) — classic but often subtle.
**Friction rub** is triphasic (atrial systole, ventricular systole, early diastole), best heard at the LLSB with the patient leaning forward at end-expiration.
First-line: **NSAID + colchicine** — colchicine reduces recurrence; glucocorticoids increase recurrence and are reserved for refractory or autoimmune cases.
**Dressler syndrome** = autoimmune pericarditis 2–10 weeks post-MI; early fibrinous pericarditis (1–3 days post-MI) is a different entity from direct inflammation over the infarct.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Pericardial disease — Acute pericarditis' section.
